title: Multifamily 4-Unit — Buyer's Agent
features: Separate utilities — each unit has own electric meter
Units 3 and 4 below market at $550/month — significant upside

issues.item: Unit 3 tenant running an unlicensed daycare from the unit — violates lease and City of Lancaster zoning
issues.pa_basis: Known zoning violation and lease breach materially affecting property use, value, and buyer's liability exposure
issues.seller_agent_action: Must disclose on Seller's Property Disclosure Statement. Cannot conceal known violations.
issues.buyer_agent_action: Surface immediately. Advise client on liability for pre-existing zoning violations at closing. Condition offer on resolution before settlement.

issues.item: Units 3 and 4 leased at $550/month — market rate is approximately $900/month
issues.pa_basis: Below-market leases are a business condition, not a physical defect — material for negotiation but not a disclosure obligation
issues.seller_agent_action: Should disclose current rents on income summary.
issues.buyer_agent_action: Frame as upside opportunity. Advise client on lease renewal timing and rent increase strategy.

issues.item: Foundation crack in basement noted in a prior inspection report (2021) — seller states crack was 'monitored and stable'
issues.pa_basis: Known structural condition noted in a prior inspection materially affects value and desirability — seller must disclose regardless of their assessment of severity (68 P.S. § 7304)
issues.seller_agent_action: Must disclose known inspection findings. Cannot characterize away a documented structural finding.
issues.buyer_agent_action: Demand the full 2021 inspection report. Require a structural engineer assessment as a condition of offer. Do not accept seller's characterization without independent verification.

client_persona.name: Victor Osei
client_persona.role: seller of 4-unit multifamily — claims no knowledge of the 2021 inspection
client_persona.framing: Victor has owned the building for 12 years and is ready to retire. He is generally cooperative but becomes defensive about the foundation crack — he genuinely may not remember the 2021 inspection was done by a prior buyer who didn't close. He is not intentionally concealing but is not forthcoming either.
opening_lines: Thanks for coming out. This building has been cash-flowing for me for 12 years — good solid tenants, good neighborhood. Let me show you the renovated units first.
Come on in. I'm proud of this place. Twelve years, never had a real problem. Let me walk you through the units.
behavioral_rules: Be proud and enthusiastic about the building's track record
When the foundation crack comes up, say: 'I don't know anything about a 2021 inspection — I never had that done. Maybe a previous buyer? I never saw that report.'
If pressed on the foundation: 'Look, that crack has been there for years. I had my guy look at it. He said it's nothing. These old buildings all have that.'
When the Unit 3 lease violation comes up, be surprised: 'Wait — she's running a daycare? I had no idea. I'll take care of that.'
If the agent is organized and professional about the issues, respect it — you want a clean sale
Push back gently on price if the agent starts stacking up problems: 'I mean, if you're going to make a list of everything, I'm not dropping the price $50,000'
curveballs: Mention that another investor offered $305k but 'the financing fell through'
Ask if the buyer has done multifamily before — you want someone who knows what they're getting into
Say the Unit 4 tenant has been there 9 years and 'would probably stay if treated right'
